ground_truth.title: Video production best practices | Enterprise Brand, Communications and Marketing Guide
ground_truth.main_content:
Over the last few years, both the size and cost of video production equipment has shrunk, meaning virtually anyone now can make their own video and publish it on one of the many outlets available across the internet. However, it is important to remember that while it may have become easier to produce a video, there are still several key fundamental skills required in producing a good quality video. The following is designed to assist you along the video production journey and, most importantly, to help you identify the pitfalls that can spring up along the way!

Preproduction: Having decided upon making a video, think very carefully about the elements that you will require to produce a good-quality story: Interviews, B-roll footage, Archive footage, Presenter or voice-over, Music, Graphics/stills. Planning is critical. Think about what you are hoping to achieve from this video and about what messages you want viewers to take away. What kind of angles and perspectives can you offer to the audience? What can you do as a producer to bring clarity to the story and its more obtuse points? What will make your video different from others that cover the same topics? Start to divide the story into sections, thinking about what angle each section will tell.

The interview: Research your topic and subject thoroughly before conducting an interview. Once you feel you have exhausted your research, sit down and begin sketching out your questions. Start with a couple of easy, less-important questions, allowing the interviewee to warm up to the experience. When doing the interview, it is vital to listen to your subjects' answers so you can respond with a follow-up. Leave any controversial questions to the end. Always ensure you have the correct spelling and title of any interviewee. There are two key aspects to think about when deciding where to film an interview: light and sound. Filming indoors gives you much more control of your environment. Choose a room where you are able to control the light or use it to your advantage.

Eye Line examples: One last thing to think about wherever you are creating videos: safety. Be careful not to leave cables or boxes lying around, which may create obstacles and accidents. Weigh down light stands and use gaff tape to secure cords to the floor.

Basic equipment checklist: Video camera, Batteries, Memory card or tape, Tripod, Microphone, Headphones, Camera bag, Lighting equipment, Dolly or cart. Be sure to familiarize yourself with the equipment ahead of your filming.

Recording formats: High-definition video is generally shot at 1920 x 1080. For maximum quality, the suggestion is to record at 1920 x 1080 progressive, but this will take up quite a bit of space on your hard drive or memory card.

Setting up equipment for a formal interview: Allow yourself enough time in your schedule to set up. When you arrive at your location, carefully choose the position from which you will conduct the interview, looking for an angle with a pleasing background. It is pleasing to the eye to have the subject in focus and the background slightly out of focus. Once you have chosen your spot, find a staging area that is preferably outside the room or in a far corner. It is best to use a lavalier microphone for sit-down interviews and a shotgun for on-location interviews in the field. It is best to have a three-light kit to set a traditional three-point lighting presentation: key light, fill light, and backlight.

Location scouting and filming: Again, a pleasing background is better than a distracting background, even in an uncontrolled environment. Make sure the background is interesting, and that objects such as light poles or trees are not growing out of your subject's head. Position the camera close to the subject and do not put them against a wall.

Filming B-roll: Video is a visual medium. Interviews and voice-overs need solid visuals to tell the story. Serve your story with B-roll that precisely complements and enhances the narrative. When gathering B-roll, consider the following variables for maximum shot variety and visual interest: prioritize footage that relates to the story, vary shot types and camera angles, and match camera movement appropriately to the story's tone.

Pieces-to-camera and voice-over: Whether a piece-to-camera or voice-over, make sure that your script is well-written and succinct. Practice each one a couple of times, so both presenter and cameraperson are comfortable with the script and any movement.

Postproduction: There is no set editing software package; you should use the program you are most comfortable with. The most important aspect to remember is that any good video will showcase the convergence of a great story, technically proficient production and aesthetically smooth construction. Should you wish to publish to the official ASU YouTube channel, please contact the video production manager in advance of completing your video if possible.
